title: Scheduled Jobs Built In On The Event Substrate Semantic Contract
description: Source-backed draft semantic contract for the scheduled jobs built in on the event substrate capability, recovered from historical source-lineage evidence as lineage. Facets cite verified source-import manifest rows; proof is hermetic/runtime lineage, not fresh USF proof.
aiGuidance: Source-backed draft. Facet descriptions are concise source-backed summaries citing verified manifest rows; proof is hermetic/stale, not current readiness. Enrich facet detail and publish fresh proof before generation; respect the hexagonal constraint in ADR 0005.

lifecycle.description: An operator creates a schedule, the enabled job persists, a due tick enqueues a platform event, a same-window tick deduplicates so no duplicate event is enqueued, pausing disables due enqueue, run-now enqueues an event immediately, and an operator patch toggles the enabled flag. Scheduled dispatch is built directly on the event substrate, so an enqueued job becomes an ordinary platform_events record (for example a report.run event) carried by the durable-queue retry and dead-letter machinery. The daemon tick is invocable even though the continuous daemon loop is not claimed wired.

stateModel.description: State is the scheduled_jobs table holding organisationId, jobKey, eventType, intervalSeconds, an enabled flag, nextRunAt, and lastRunAt, together with an event idempotency window that prevents duplicate enqueue within the same due window. Enqueued jobs materialise as platform_events on the shared event substrate, so downstream state, ordering, retry, and dead-letter follow the event-bus state model. Row-level security scopes scheduled jobs to the owning organisation.

permissions.description: Scheduled-job routes are global, operator-only platform surfaces: listing requires platform.jobs.read while create, run-now, and patch require platform.jobs.write. The due tick is server-internal and is not callable from tenant UI. Tenant isolation is enforced by organisationId scoping and row-level security so one organisation cannot list or trigger another organisation's jobs.

contracts.description: The contract surface is GET and POST /api/admin/scheduled-jobs, POST /api/admin/scheduled-jobs/:jobId/run, and PATCH /api/admin/scheduled-jobs/:jobId, with the periodic due tick exposed only as a server-internal operation behind the EventBusPort and PostgresEventBusAdapter. Enqueue is expressed as event publication, so the scheduled-job contract composes with the event-bus contract rather than duplicating delivery semantics. The port seam keeps the substrate provider swappable.

validation.description: List routes require organisationId, invalid job ids are rejected, create validates the schedule body, run-now validates the job id, and the same-window idempotency window prevents duplicate event enqueue for a job that has already fired in the current window. Disabled jobs are never enqueued by the due tick. These invariants are exercised by the scheduled-jobs and scheduled-job-routes runtime proofs.

errorModel.description: Outcomes are deterministic and fail closed: a missing organisationId returns 400, an invalid job id returns 400, a disabled job is simply not due, a repeated due tick within the window is deduplicated, and an unavailable Postgres substrate yields an honest skip or readiness failure rather than a fabricated enqueue. Errors are typed and tenant-scoped and never silently drop scheduled work.

auditModel.description: scheduled_job.set and scheduled_job.run are audit-before-change operations, so the audit record is written before the schedule mutation or manual run takes effect. The recurring due-tick enqueue is represented operationally by the resulting platform_events records rather than as a separate user-mutation audit event. This keeps operator intent auditable while treating routine dispatch as operational telemetry.

readinessModel.description: Readiness is that the due tick is invocable and the routes are accessible, even though the continuous daemon loop is not claimed; the live proof validates the table schema, row-level-security isolation, due/run-now enqueue behaviour, and the route handlers, and degrades explicitly when the substrate is unavailable. Because dispatch rides the event substrate, scheduled-job readiness inherits the event-bus degraded posture and never reports production-shaped behaviour as production-live.

proof.description: Behaviour is exercised by two runtime proofs: scheduled-jobs validates live Postgres schedule persistence, due and run-now enqueue, row-level security, and the no-secret schema, and scheduled-job-routes validates the route handlers and access metadata, described in the corpus as level-4 live substrate and route validation. The proofs are the lineage evidence, not a USF readiness claim. Proof is hermetic/runtime lineage; fresh USF proof pending USF-101/USF-59.

uiSemanticDefinition.description: No verified UI source path in the manifest yet; author the UI semantic model before generation.
